title_en: Understanding Sadness and Depression in Islam
title_id: Memahami Kesedihan dan Depresi dalam Islam
content_en:
The experience of sadness, grief, and psychological suffering is universally human — it does not discriminate by faith, virtue, or worldly status. Aaidh al-Qarni opens his celebrated work with an honest acknowledgment of this reality: sadness and depression afflict the pious and the sinful alike, the wealthy and the poor, the scholar and the ordinary person. Islam does not promise its followers immunity from these experiences, but it offers resources for understanding and navigating them that are both philosophically deep and practically powerful.

The Quran itself is filled with the experience of sadness. The Prophet Ibrahim cried with longing for the land of his mission. Yaqub (Jacob) wept so intensely for his lost son Yusuf that he lost his sight — the Quran describes his sorrow without condemning it. Musa (Moses) felt anxiety and fear on multiple occasions. The Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, experienced such profound grief at the deaths of his beloved wife Khadijah and his uncle Abu Talib that the scholars named that year 'The Year of Sorrow.' Grief, in the Islamic tradition, is not a spiritual failure but a human experience that even the prophets underwent.

Al-Qarni draws a careful distinction between the sadness and grief that are normal human responses to loss, disappointment, and difficulty — and the prolonged, debilitating sadness that prevents a person from fulfilling their obligations and engaging with life. The former is acknowledged by Islam as natural and even appropriate in many circumstances; the latter is a condition that requires active intervention using the spiritual and practical tools that Islam provides. The goal of 'Don't Be Sad' is not to prevent all sadness — an impossible and undesirable aspiration — but to prevent unnecessary sadness and to provide tools for emerging from unavoidable sadness with one's faith and functionality intact.

The Islamic understanding of sadness is also distinguished by its theological framework. Sadness that stems from worldly loss — of wealth, status, loved ones, health — is in the Islamic view a trial (ibtila') that has a divine purpose the human being may not immediately perceive. The Quran declares: 'We will surely test you with something of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth and lives and fruits, but give good tidings to the patient' (Al-Baqarah 2:155-157). This framing transforms the experience of loss from random misfortune into meaningful encounter with divine wisdom — a transformation that is itself one of the most powerful antidotes to despair.

Al-Qarni also addresses the sources of much unnecessary human sadness: excessive anxiety about an uncertain future, prolonged dwelling on painful memories of the past, unhealthy comparison with those who appear better off, and the habit of imagining disasters that have not yet occurred and may never occur. The Prophet's specific dua seeking protection from anxiety (hamm) and grief (huzn) — 'O Allah, I seek refuge in You from anxiety and grief, from weakness and laziness...' — acknowledges these as real afflictions while offering the Muslim a practical tool for addressing them.

The opening chapter establishes the book's fundamental premise: that sadness, while real and sometimes severe, is not the final word about the human condition. Allah has not created the human being for sadness but for joy — the ultimate joy of His presence and pleasure — and He has provided, through the Quran and Sunnah, a comprehensive guide for finding the path from sadness to the genuine peace that is available even in the most difficult circumstances.
content_id:
Pengalaman kesedihan, duka, dan penderitaan psikologis adalah universally manusiawi — ia tidak membeda-bedakan berdasarkan iman, kebajikan, atau status duniawi. Aaidh al-Qarni membuka karyanya yang terkenal dengan pengakuan jujur atas realitas ini: kesedihan dan depresi menimpa orang yang saleh dan yang berdosa, orang kaya dan orang miskin, ulama dan orang biasa. Islam tidak menjanjikan kekebalan para pengikutnya dari pengalaman-pengalaman ini, tetapi menawarkan sumber-sumber untuk memahami dan menavigasinya yang keduanya secara filosofis mendalam dan secara praktis kuat.

Al-Quran sendiri dipenuhi dengan pengalaman kesedihan. Nabi Ibrahim menangis dengan kerinduan untuk tanah misinya. Ya'qub (Yakub) menangis begitu intens untuk putranya Yusuf yang hilang sehingga ia kehilangan penglihatannya — Al-Quran menggambarkan kesedihannya tanpa mengecamnya. Musa merasakan kecemasan dan ketakutan pada berbagai kesempatan. Nabi Muhammad ﷺ mengalami duka yang begitu mendalam atas kematian istri tercintanya Khadijah dan pamannya Abu Thalib sehingga para ulama menamai tahun itu 'Tahun Kesedihan.' Duka, dalam tradisi Islam, bukanlah kegagalan spiritual tetapi pengalaman manusiawi yang bahkan para nabi pun melewatinya.

Al-Qarni menarik perbedaan yang cermat antara kesedihan dan duka yang merupakan respons manusiawi normal terhadap kehilangan, kekecewaan, dan kesulitan — dan kesedihan yang berkepanjangan dan melemahkan yang menghalangi seseorang dari memenuhi kewajibannya dan terlibat dengan kehidupan. Yang pertama diakui oleh Islam sebagai alami dan bahkan tepat dalam banyak keadaan; yang terakhir adalah kondisi yang memerlukan intervensi aktif menggunakan alat-alat spiritual dan praktis yang Islam sediakan. Tujuan 'Jangan Bersedih' bukan untuk mencegah semua kesedihan — aspirasi yang tidak mungkin dan tidak diinginkan — tetapi untuk mencegah kesedihan yang tidak perlu dan menyediakan alat-alat untuk keluar dari kesedihan yang tidak dapat dihindari dengan iman dan fungsi seseorang tetap utuh.

Pemahaman Islam tentang kesedihan juga dibedakan oleh kerangka teologisnya. Kesedihan yang berasal dari kehilangan duniawi — kekayaan, status, orang-orang tercinta, kesehatan — dalam pandangan Islam adalah ujian (ibtila') yang memiliki tujuan ilahi yang mungkin tidak segera dipahami manusia. Al-Quran menyatakan: 'Dan Kami pasti akan menguji kamu dengan sedikit ketakutan, kelaparan, kekurangan harta, jiwa, dan buah-buahan. Dan sampaikanlah kabar gembira kepada orang-orang yang sabar' (Al-Baqarah 2:155-157). Framing ini mengubah pengalaman kehilangan dari kemalangan acak menjadi pertemuan bermakna dengan kebijaksanaan ilahi — sebuah transformasi yang sendirinya merupakan salah satu penawar paling kuat terhadap keputusasaan.

Al-Qarni juga membahas sumber-sumber dari banyak kesedihan manusia yang tidak perlu: kecemasan berlebihan tentang masa depan yang tidak pasti, penghunian berkepanjangan pada kenangan menyakitkan dari masa lalu, perbandingan tidak sehat dengan mereka yang tampak lebih baik, dan kebiasaan membayangkan bencana-bencana yang belum terjadi dan mungkin tidak pernah terjadi. Doa Nabi ﷺ yang spesifik memohon perlindungan dari kecemasan (hamm) dan kesedihan (huzn) — 'Ya Allah, aku berlindung kepada-Mu dari kecemasan dan kesedihan, dari kelemahan dan kemalasan...' — mengakui ini sebagai penyakit-penyakit yang nyata sambil menawarkan umat Muslim alat praktis untuk mengatasinya.

Bab pembuka menetapkan premis fundamental buku ini: bahwa kesedihan, meskipun nyata dan kadang-kadang berat, bukanlah kata terakhir tentang kondisi manusia. Allah tidak menciptakan manusia untuk kesedihan tetapi untuk kegembiraan — kegembiraan tertinggi dari hadirat dan keridhaan-Nya — dan Dia telah menyediakan, melalui Al-Quran dan Sunnah, panduan komprehensif untuk menemukan jalan dari kesedihan menuju kedamaian sejati yang tersedia bahkan dalam keadaan-keadaan yang paling sulit sekalipun.
content_ar: The experience of الحزن, الحزن, and psychological المعاناة is universally human — it does not discriminate by الإيمان, الفضيلة, or worldly status. Aaidh al-Qarni opens his celebrated work with an honest acknowledgment of this reality: الحزن and الاكتئاب afflict the pious and the sinful alike, the wealthy and the poor, the scholar and the ordinary person. الإسلام does not promise its followers immunity from these experiences, but it offers resources for الفهم and navigating them that are both philosophically deep and practically powerful. The القرآن الكريم itself is filled with the experience of الحزن. النبي ﷺ إبراهيم ﷺ cried with longing for the land of his mission. Yaqub (Jacob) wept لذا intensely for his lost son يوسف ﷺ that he lost his sight — the القرآن الكريم describes his sorrow without condemning it. موسى ﷺ (موسى) felt القلق and الخوف on multiple occasions. النبي ﷺ محمد ﷺ, ﷺ, experienced such profound الحزن at the deaths of his beloved الزوجة Khadijah and his uncle Abu Talib that the العلماء named that year 'The Year of Sorrow.' الحزن, in the Islamic التراث, is not a spiritual الخسارة but a human experience that even the الأنبياء underwent. Al-Qarni draws a careful distinction between the الحزن and الحزن that are normal human responses to loss, disappointment, and difficulty — and the prolonged, debilitating الحزن that prevents a person from fulfilling their obligations and engaging with الحياة. The former is acknowledged by الإسلام as natural and even appropriate in many circumstances; the latter is a condition that requires active intervention using the spiritual and practical tools that الإسلام provides. The goal of 'Don't Be Sad' is not to prevent all الحزن — an impossible and undesirable aspiration — but to prevent unnecessary الحزن and to provide tools for emerging from unavoidable الحزن with one's الإيمان and functionality intact. The Islamic الفهم of الحزن is أيضاً distinguished by its theological framework. الحزن that stems from worldly loss — of الثروة, status, loved ones, الصحة — is in the Islamic view a الابتلاء (ibtila') that has a divine purpose the human being may not immediately perceive. The القرآن الكريم declares: 'We will surely الاختبار you with something of الخوف and hunger and a loss of الثروة and lives and fruits, but give الخير tidings to the patient' (Al-Baqarah 2:155-157). This framing transforms the experience of loss from random misfortune into meaningful encounter with divine الحكمة — a transformation that is itself one of the most powerful antidotes to despair. Al-Qarni أيضاً addresses the sources of much unnecessary human الحزن: excessive القلق about an uncertain future, prolonged dwelling on painful memories of the past, unhealthy comparison with those who appear better off, and the habit of imagining disasters that have not yet occurred and may never occur. النبي ﷺ's specific الدعاء seeking protection from القلق (hamm) and الحزن (huzn) — 'O الله, I seek refuge in You from القلق and الحزن, from الضعف and laziness...' — acknowledges these as real afflictions بينما offering the مسلم a practical tool for addressing them. The opening الباب establishes the الكتاب's الأساسي premise: that الحزن, بينما real and sometimes severe, is not the final word about the human condition. الله has not created the human being for الحزن but for joy — the ultimate joy of His presence and pleasure — and He has provided, through the القرآن الكريم and السنة, a comprehensive guide for finding the الطريق from الحزن to the genuine peace that is available even in the most difficult circumstances.
